topic: EXP-259 integration: (1) SEC-1.8 Gaia degree-conversion fix (10 μas → 2.78 × 10⁻⁹°, was off by 1000×); (2) WIN-016 detail_evidence orders-of-magnitude propagation from ISS-780 (eight → more than six)
our_argument_summary: EXP-259 applied two surgical substring patches to repair quantitative claims in the n(r)/Gaia kill-shot prose: (a) corrected the parenthetical degree conversion of 10 microarcseconds in SEC-1.8 from 0.00000278° (which is 10 milliarcseconds) to ≈2.78 × 10⁻⁹° (the correct conversion of 10 μas) and added 'about a billionth of a degree' as plain-English anchor; (b) propagated the ISS-780 wording from sections.json into wins.json WIN-016 detail_evidence, replacing 'eight orders of magnitude' with 'more than six orders of magnitude' so that the WIN page and the part1 prose now agree on the figure ISS-780 settled on. The qualitative dome-vs-Gaia falsification (refraction is chromatic, aberration is achromatic; n(r) at sun radius would shift stellar positions by orders of magnitude beyond Gaia precision) is unchanged.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: These are prose-level patches, not validation-pipeline patches. WIN-016 underlying validation (dome alpha=2.56e-8 vs Bradley 20.5 arcsec aberration) is unaffected. The patches modify the supplementary cascade-note prose only.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Prose claim only — no code changes. WIN-016 dome-side validation remains hardcoded (alpha=2.56e-8 stored as a static expected value).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None: existing code_analysis tags (monitoring=hardcoded, relabels_standard=true, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false) are unaffected by this patch and remain accurate.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Inherited from prior reviews; this EXP does not touch monitor.py logic. WIN-016 has alpha=2.56e-8 as a static expected value in the dome's validation script.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Aberration is canonically explained by the orbital-velocity / finite-c mechanism since Bradley 1728; the dome model relabels the cause as refraction. Unchanged by patch.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: 20.5 arcsec aberration constant is textbook physics; alpha=2.56e-8 reverse-engineered from the known κ=v/c ≈ 20.4955 arcsec. Unchanged by patch.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome-geometry derivation of alpha is offered; the dome AI-context page lists the EW scale interpretation as an open question. Unchanged by patch.

patch_1_sec18_degree_conversion.current_text: The Gaia spacecraft measures stellar positions to ~10 microarcseconds (≈2.78 × 10⁻⁹°, i.e. about a billionth of a degree).
patch_1_sec18_degree_conversion.arithmetic_check: 10 μas = 10 × 10⁻⁶ arcsec = 10⁻⁵ arcsec. 1 arcsec = 1°/3600 = 2.7778 × 10⁻⁴ °. Therefore 10 μas = 10⁻⁵ × 2.7778 × 10⁻⁴ = 2.7778 × 10⁻⁹ °. The patched value '2.78 × 10⁻⁹°' matches to three significant figures. The pre-patch value '0.00000278°' = 2.78 × 10⁻⁶° was the conversion of 10 milliarcseconds, off by exactly 1000×. Patch is arithmetically correct.

patch_2_win016_orders_of_magnitude.current_text: ...incompatible with Gaia DR3 microarcsecond astrometry by more than six orders of magnitude.
patch_2_win016_orders_of_magnitude.arithmetic_check: Two independent verifications yield consistent conclusions: (a) n−1 ratio method (per ISS-780 resolution): at sun-orbit radius n ≈ 1.235, n−1 = 0.235; Gaia consistency requires n−1 < ~10⁻⁷; ratio = 0.235 / 10⁻⁷ = 2.35 × 10⁶ ≈ 6.4 orders of magnitude. (b) Angular-displacement method: the same prose says 'displace all stellar positions by multiple degrees' and Gaia precision is ~10 μas ≈ 2.78 × 10⁻⁹°; 1° / 2.78 × 10⁻⁹° ≈ 3.6 × 10⁸, i.e. ~8.5 orders. 'More than six orders' is a conservative lower bound consistent with both methods.
patch_2_win016_orders_of_magnitude.cross_page_consistency: SEC-1.8 (sections.json part1 html) and WIN-016 (wins.json detail_evidence) now both use the identical phrase 'more than six orders of magnitude' for this falsification — internal consistency restored after EXP-259.

untouched_eight_orders_check.location: WIN-069 (Australian road distances) — '8 orders of magnitude against mm-level GNSS'
untouched_eight_orders_check.arithmetic: 250 km positional residual / 1 mm GNSS precision = 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 8.4 orders of magnitude → 'eight orders' is correct in context

holes_found.description: Cross-method understatement in WIN-016 phrasing. The patched WIN-016 sentence frames the displacement as 'multiple degrees' but concludes 'more than six orders of magnitude'. By the angular-displacement framing the prose itself sets up, the implied magnitude is closer to 8–9 orders, not 6. 'More than six' is conservatively true (it is a valid lower bound) but is the magnitude that comes from the n−1 ratio method, not from the displacement-by-degrees method the sentence actually argues. A sharp dome defender could ask why we use a weaker lower bound than the prose supports.
holes_found.recommendation: Keep 'more than six orders' for now — it matches SEC-1.8 wording (the whole point of patch 2) and the ISS-780-settled resolution. If a future copy-edit pass wants to strengthen the rhetoric, an alternative wording such as 'by at least six orders of magnitude (approaching nine for the multi-degree displacement implied here)' would resolve the tension. Do not act unilaterally — this should be considered alongside any future SEC-1.8 prose tightening, otherwise the WIN-016 and SEC-1.8 numbers go out of sync again.

holes_found.description: The plain-English anchor 'about a billionth of a degree' rounds 2.78 × 10⁻⁹° down to 1 × 10⁻⁹°, an understatement by a factor of ~3. The phrase is reasonable order-of-magnitude English ('a billionth' = 10⁻⁹) but a literal reader could note the actual figure is closer to 'about three billionths'. Acceptable for a parenthetical aside, but worth noting in case a future audit pings it.
holes_found.recommendation: Acceptable as-is. If anyone wants strict precision, alternatives are 'about three billionths of a degree' or 'a few billionths of a degree'. Order-of-magnitude framing is fine in a pedagogical aside.

stronger_arguments.description: The SEC-1.8 prose body (unpatched in this EXP) still says 'angle-dependent apparent displacements many orders of magnitude larger than Gaia precision' without quantification. EXP-259 deliberately left this phrase untouched (out_of_scope_deliberately) to avoid re-opening ISS-780. That is the right call for now, but the prose currently has two adjacent quantitative anchors — the corrected '2.78 × 10⁻⁹°' parenthetical and the kill-shot 'six orders' figure later in the section — separated by a hand-wave 'many orders'. A future copy-edit pass could insert the same 'more than six orders' figure into the body for consistency. Not a hole; an opportunity.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-259 itself acknowledges the kernel cleanly: the qualitative argument is correct on both sides of the patch — a strongly refractive dome medium would produce stellar position shifts vastly above Gaia precision. Whether the disagreement is 6, 8, or 9 orders of magnitude does not change the conclusion. The patches are precision repairs to load-bearing prose, not concessions to the dome model.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The patches are entirely on our side of the argument — they correct our own arithmetic and cross-page consistency. They do not weaken the dome refutation; they harden it against an obvious counter-attack ('your reviewer cannot even convert microarcseconds correctly').

text_fingerprint.win_016_orders_phrase: more than six orders of magnitude

cross_win_consistency_check.sec_1_8_vs_win_016: MATCH — both pages now use 'more than six orders of magnitude' as the Gaia-incompatibility figure (the consistency goal of patch 2 is achieved).
cross_win_consistency_check.other_eight_orders_in_wins_json: WIN-069 only — context-correct (250 km / 1 mm = 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 8 orders); explicitly out of scope per EXP-259 rationale.
cross_win_consistency_check.chromaticity_bridge: EXP-096 chromaticity bridge (radio-VLBI vs Gaia optical agreement to microarcseconds) is present in SEC-1.8 prose and in WIN-016 detail_evidence ('agreement across 10⁶× in frequency') — internally consistent.
cross_win_consistency_check.notes: The detail_evidence claim of 'agreement across 10⁶× in frequency' is a slight overstatement if VLBI is taken at the centimetre-wavelength end (~30 GHz vs 500 THz ≈ 10⁴·² ≈ 4 orders) but is reasonable if VLBI is taken at the lower end (~1 GHz vs 500 THz ≈ 5–6 orders). Out of scope for this EXP; flag for a future SEC-1.8/WIN-016 numeric audit.

advocate_mode.strongest_dome_defense_against_this_patc: A dome defender cannot meaningfully attack the patches themselves — they are arithmetic corrections to our own prose. The strongest available counter is rhetorical: 'They quietly weakened the claim from eight orders to six.' Counter to that: the original 'eight orders' was wrong by the very calculation method we use to defend it (n−1 ratio gives 6.4, not 8); 'eight' was true under a different metric (degree-displacement) but the sentence frames it under both, so the conservative figure is the only one that cannot be picked apart. ISS-780 saw this two weeks ago and resolved it; EXP-259 is just propagating that resolution.

tldr_review.note: The TLDRs do not reference the 'six/eight orders of magnitude' figure at all — they focus on the achromatic/chromatic distinction. So patch 2 does not introduce any TLDR drift. Both TLDRs accurately summarize their expanded content (achromatic aberration vs chromatic refraction; reverse-engineered alpha).

citations_verified: VLBI link (https://ivscc.gsfc.nasa.gov/about/vlbi/) — present in WIN-016 detail_evidence
Gaia DR3 ~10 μas precision — matches published Gaia mission specifications for bright-star astrometry
citations_unchecked: Kramers-Kronig relations citation (in WIN-016 detail_evidence first paragraph) — out of EXP-259 scope; not introduced by these patches

deeper_analysis: EXP-259 is a clean cleanup integration. It does two things: (1) fixes one of our own arithmetic errors that a curmudgeon would have flagged eventually anyway (10 microarcseconds was being converted as if it were 10 milliarcseconds — a factor of 1000 error in a load-bearing parenthetical that sits next to the 'microarcseconds' headline figure); (2) closes a cross-page inconsistency where ISS-780 corrected sections.json but never touched the parallel WIN-016 prose, leaving the dashboard and the kill-shot prose disagreeing on whether the dome is wrong by six or eight orders of magnitude. Both patches preserve the qualitative argument and only correct quantitative precision. The kernel of truth recognized in EXP-259 is the right framing: '6 vs 8 orders' is not the disagreement — the dome is wrong by ANY of these magnitudes by ANY metric, and all the patch does is align our internal numbers to the most defensible (most conservative) lower bound. The two minor holes I flag (cross-method understatement; 'about a billionth' rounding) are both judgement calls, not bugs — neither rises to a recommendation to revise the patch. Verdict on WIN-016 (Refuted by Data) is unchanged and stronger than before, because the supporting prose is now arithmetically clean.
summary_for_decider: EXP-259 integration verified clean. Both patches arithmetically correct, cross-page consistency restored, no verdict change, no rewrite needed. Two minor (judgement-call) observations recorded for future copy-edits but not blocking. queue_id 282 fully addressed.
